sections.header: Verses 1-8 — "The LORD Is a Jealous and Avenging God"

heb.paragraph: The adjective qanna describes YHWH's exclusive claim on covenant loyalty. It derives from the root qnʾ, conveying passionate zeal that will not tolerate rivals. In the Decalogue (Exod 20:5; 34:14), divine jealousy is the basis for prohibiting idolatry. Nahum deploys this term at the outset to frame Nineveh's destruction as the consequence of violating the cosmic order over which YHWH exercises sole sovereignty.

The participle noqem occurs three times in 1:2, an extraordinary rhetorical concentration. The root nqm denotes the execution of retributive justice, not capricious revenge. In ancient Near Eastern treaty contexts, the suzerain's vengeance against a rebellious vassal was a legal obligation. YHWH's threefold vengeance establishes the juridical framework for the entire oracle: Nineveh's destruction is warranted by covenant law.

refs.note: The foundational divine self-revelation describes YHWH as 'slow to anger' yet not clearing the guilty — the same tension Nahum exploits in 1:2-3, applying covenant theology to Assyria's judgment.
David's theophanic psalm describes God's descent with earthquake, storm, and rebuke of the waters — imagery Nahum draws upon for the cosmic scope of YHWH's intervention against Nineveh.
The 'feet of one who brings good news' in Nahum 1:15 directly parallels Isaiah's herald of peace, linking Nineveh's fall to the restoration of Zion.

notes.note: The superscription designates this as an oracle (massa) specifically against Nineveh, the capital of the Assyrian Empire that had terrorized the ancient world for centuries. Unlike Jonah, who was sent to Nineveh with a message of potential repentance, Nahum delivers an unconditional sentence of destruction. The time for mercy has passed.

The threefold repetition of vengeance in verse 2 is not petulant rage but the judicial prerogative of the sovereign Lord. God is slow to anger (cf. Exod 34:6), but slowness to anger is not absence of anger. When divine patience is exhausted by persistent impenitence, judgment is certain and thorough.

The cosmic disturbances — quaking mountains, melting hills, trembling earth — reveal that Nineveh faces not merely a political enemy but the Creator of the universe. No fortress, however impregnable, can withstand the One before whom the very rocks shatter.

This verse is the theological heart of the chapter. The same God who destroys the wicked is a refuge (maʻoz) for the righteous. Divine judgment and divine goodness are not contradictory attributes but complementary expressions of the same holy character.

God's vengeance is here distinguished from human vengeance by its union with patience. Men rush to avenge themselves from passion; God delays from forbearance, yet his delay renders the final stroke more terrible. The threefold repetition teaches that God's justice is no cold abstraction but a burning zeal for his own glory.

The prophet sets side by side the goodness and the severity of God — not as contradictions but as the two faces of the same sovereign will. He is a stronghold to those who trust, and an overwhelming flood to those who resist. The distinction lies not in God but in the posture of the creature before him.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The Hebrew massa can mean 'oracle' or 'burden,' suggesting both the prophetic message and the weight of judgment it carries. The dual designation as both massa and hazon (vision) is unique among the prophetic superscriptions.

The LXX renders noqem with ekdikon (avenger), emphasizing the legal-forensic dimension. The threefold occurrence in a single verse creates an emphatic rhetorical structure unparalleled in the prophetic corpus.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The acrostic structure of the opening hymn, while partial and debated, places this oracle within Israel's liturgical tradition. The verbal connections to the Sinai theophany (Exod 19:16-18) and the Song of the Sea (Exod 15) ground Nahum's message in the foundational events of Israelite faith. The God who conquered Pharaoh will now conquer Assyria.

The juxtaposition of refuge (maʻoz) and flood (sheteph) in adjacent verses creates a deliberate theological contrast. The same divine power that overwhelms the wicked protects the faithful. Nahum thus stands within the Deuteronomic tradition of blessing and curse (Deut 28-30), applied now to the international arena.

hist.context: Nahum's superscription identifies the book as both a massa ("oracle" or "burden") and a "vision" (hazon), combining prophetic authority with visionary revelation. The opening hymn (1:2-8) is a partial acrostic poem tracing roughly half the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through kaph), drawing on theophanic traditions reminiscent of Deuteronomy 33, Judges 5, and Psalm 18. The imagery of YHWH rebuking the sea, withering Bashan and Carmel, and melting mountains echoes the Divine Warrior motif deeply embedded in Israelite liturgical tradition. The pivot at verse 7 — 'The LORD is good, a refuge in times of trouble' — introduces the crucial theological distinction: YHWH's wrath is not indiscriminate but discriminating, destroying enemies while sheltering the faithful. The 'overwhelming flood' of verse 8 may allude to the historical tradition that the Khosr River's flooding contributed to Nineveh's fall in 612 BC.

heb.gloss: herald / one who brings good news
heb.paragraph: The participle mevasser (from basar, 'to bring news') in 1:15 denotes a messenger announcing victory. This term carries deep theological weight: in Isaiah 40:9 and 52:7, the mevasser announces YHWH's return to Zion and the end of exile. Nahum appropriates the term to declare that Nineveh's destruction is itself the 'good news' that liberates Judah from Assyrian oppression.

refs.note: The destruction of Sennacherib's army before Jerusalem and his subsequent assassination fulfill the pattern Nahum describes: the one who plotted evil against the LORD meets a decisive end.

Paul quotes the 'beautiful feet' tradition (combining Isa 52:7 and Nah 1:15) to describe the preaching of the gospel, extending the herald motif from Nineveh's political destruction to eschatological salvation.

notes.note: The rhetorical question 'Whatever they plot against the LORD he will bring to an end' captures a principle that governs all of history: no human scheme can ultimately prevail against divine sovereignty. Sennacherib plotted, Pharaoh plotted, Haman plotted — all were brought to nothing.

The shift to direct comfort for Judah reveals God's dual purpose in judgment: the destruction of the oppressor is simultaneously the liberation of the oppressed. The yoke and shackles are Assyrian imperial domination, which God himself will shatter.
The finality of this decree is sobering: 'You will have no descendants to bear your name.' When God determines to end a dynasty or empire, the sentence is irrevocable. Assyria would never rise again.

The herald of peace announces that the geopolitical threat that had haunted Judah for over a century is permanently removed. The call to resume festivals implies that true worship can only flourish when the people are free from oppression.

God here speaks as a judge who has already passed sentence and now announces the execution. The comfort to Judah is inseparable from the doom of Assyria: the same decree that destroys the one delivers the other. This is the pattern of all divine judgments in history.

The prophet borrows Isaiah's image of the herald on the mountains to declare that Nineveh's fall is gospel — good news. The festivals of Judah, long disrupted by foreign domination, may now resume. Political liberation serves spiritual restoration.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The imagery of thorns, drunkenness, and stubble combines three metaphors of vulnerability and combustibility. The textual difficulties in this verse have generated numerous emendations, but the overall sense of total destruction is clear.

The verse numbering varies between English and Hebrew traditions; in the MT this verse is 2:1. The verbal parallels with Isa 52:7 are exact, suggesting either literary dependence or a shared prophetic formula.

robertson.source: A.T. Robertson, Word Pictures in the New Testament — Public Domain

notes.note: The alternation between addresses to Nineveh and Judah creates a covenantal lawsuit structure (rib). YHWH simultaneously prosecutes the guilty party and vindicates the aggrieved. The decree of verse 14 employs treaty-curse language: the termination of the dynasty's name is the ultimate covenant curse in ancient Near Eastern diplomacy.

The herald (mevasser) on the mountains stands at the intersection of prophetic and cultic traditions. The summons to resume pilgrimage festivals (haggim) implies that Assyrian hegemony had effectively suppressed Judah's covenant worship — a historical reality attested by the syncretistic practices Josiah's reforms sought to eliminate.

hist.context: Verses 9-15 shift from theophanic hymn to direct address, alternating between Nineveh (vv. 9, 11, 14) and Judah (vv. 12-13, 15). This rhetorical oscillation creates a dramatic contrast: what is doom for Assyria is liberation for Judah. The image of 'entangled thorns' and 'dry stubble' (v. 10) suggests the inextricable corruption of Nineveh's social fabric. Verse 11 likely alludes to Sennacherib, whose campaign against Judah in 701 BC was the paradigmatic act of plotting 'evil against the LORD' (cf. 2 Kgs 18-19; Isa 36-37). The command to 'celebrate your festivals' (v. 15) presupposes that Assyrian domination had disrupted Judah's cultic life. The herald on the mountains announces not merely military victory but the restoration of covenant worship.

themes.note: Chapter 1 is dominated by themes of divine judgment and sovereignty. The theophanic hymn establishes YHWH's absolute power over creation, while the alternating addresses reveal that judgment and mercy are inseparable: the same act that destroys Nineveh liberates Judah.

rows.label: vv.2-6
rows.text: Theophanic Hymn: YHWH the Divine Warrior

rows.label: v.14
rows.text: Decree Against Nineveh: End of Dynasty

lit.note: Chapter 1 alternates between a cosmic theophany (vv. 2-8) and direct oracles to Nineveh and Judah (vv. 9-15), establishing the theological framework for the entire book: YHWH's jealous wrath destroys the impenitent oppressor while providing refuge for the faithful.

ppl.name: Nahum
ppl.text: Nahum's superscription identifies the book as both a massa ("oracle" or "burden") and a "vision" (hazon), combining prophetic authority with visionary revelation…

trans.title: Comparing translations in The LORD's Vengeance and Goodness

translations.text: Who can withstand his indignation? Who can endure his fierce anger? His wrath is poured out like fire; the rocks are shattered before him.
Who can stand before his indignation? Who can endure the heat of his anger? His wrath is poured out like fire, and the rocks are broken into pieces by him.
The Lord is a jealous and avenging God; the Lord takes vengeance and is filled with wrath. The Lord takes vengeance on his foes and vents his wrath against his enemies.
The LORD is a jealous and avenging God; the LORD is avenging and wrathful; the LORD takes vengeance on his adversaries and keeps wrath for his enemies.

David's theophanic psalm describes God's descent with earthquake, storm, and rebuke of the waters — imagery Nahum draws upon for the cosmic scope of YHWH's intervention against Nineveh.
The 'feet of one who brings good news' in Nahum 1:15 directly parallels Isaiah's herald of peace, linking Nineveh's fall to the restoration of Zion.

coaching.tip: Nahum opens with a hymn to divine jealousy and vengeance (vv. 2-8) that makes modern readers uncomfortable. But context matters: Nineveh was the Assyrian capital, responsible for unmatched brutality across centuries. This isn't divine pettiness but justice delayed finally arriving. The God who is 'slow to anger' (v. 3) eventually acts.

The contrast between verse 7 ('The LORD is good, a stronghold in the day of trouble') and verse 8 ('but with an overflowing flood he will make a complete end of his adversaries') is the book's tension. God is refuge and destroyer, depending on where you stand. Nahum forces a choice: Is Nineveh's fate terrifying or comforting?
